data.title: Parnassus Value Select ETF Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

While U.S. equities broadly had a difficult quarter, value stocks posted a modest gain as measured by the Russell 1000 Value Index. After the new administration took office, post-election optimism regarding lower regulation and business-friendly policies gave way to the reality of restrictive fiscal policies and erratic tariff announcements creating uncertainty and recession fears. Volatility rose significantly, and investor sentiment shifted away from growth-oriented stocks—including many large technology names focused on semiconductors and/or artificial intelligence (AI) innovation— and toward stocks perceived as defensive. Against this risk-off backdrop, the best sector performers in the Russell 1000 Value Index were the Energy, Communication Services and Health Care sectors, while the Information Technology and Consumer Discretionary sectors performed the worst.

Performance data quoted represent past performance and are no guarantee of future returns. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted, and current performance information to the most recent month end is available on the Parnassus website (www.parnassus.com). Investment return and principal value will fluctuate, so an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original principal cost. Returns shown in the table do not reflect the deduction of taxes a shareholder may pay on fund distributions or redemption of shares. The Russell 1000 Value Index is an unmanaged index of common stocks, and it is not possible to invest directly in an index. Index figures do not take any expenses, fees or taxes into account, but mutual fund returns do. The estimated impact of

individual stocks on the Fund’s performance is provided by FactSet.

Quarterly Performance Review

Health Care holdings weighed on relative performance

The Fund returned -2.06% (net of fees) during the quarter, underperforming the Russell 1000 Value’s 2.14%. The Health Care sector was the largest detractor from relative performance due to stock selection, mitigated somewhat by a favorable overweight. Stock selection and an overweight in Information Technology had negative impacts. On the upside, stock selection in the Industrials and Financials sectors lifted relative results.

Top Quarterly Contributors

Baxter International (BAX) announced robust fourth-quarter financial results and projected full-year 2025 profit above Wall Street expectations, bolstering investor confidence in the stock. Medical device makers like Baxter are benefiting from increased demand for elective surgeries as the population ages.

Verizon Communications (VZ), which pays the highest dividend of any stock in the Dow Jones Industrial Index, drew investor interest in the quarter’s risk-off, defensively focused environment.

AstraZeneca’s (AZN)(OTCPK:AZNCF) shares rose after the company announced a favorable outcome from investigations by the Chinese government into allegations of improper drug importation, which investors had feared could result in much more costly fines.

Visa (V) reported quarterly earnings that topped consensus expectations and issued optimistic guidance at its latest Investor Day. In a market environment where investors turned to more defensive options, Visa benefited from being relatively resilient for a payments stock, given its domestic debit card exposure, which is primarily grocery-related.

Deere & Company (DE) stock gained as investors grew optimistic for a recovery in earnings amid signs of improvement in the sales cycle for agricultural equipment. Rising energy and commodity prices also buoyed the stock.

Bottom Quarterly Contributors

Bio-Rad Laboratories (BIO) shares fell during the quarter as investors reacted to multiple factors, including plans to reduce its workforce and declining sales in its Life Science division. A one-time issue around lower reimbursement from diabetes testing in China also pressured the stock.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) shares fell sharply in January following the debut of China-based DeepSeek’s AI model, which was reportedly much more inexpensively trained than other models. We remain optimistic that demand for AI semiconductors will grow despite algorithmic efficiencies.

Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) reported slower-than-expected growth for its cloud business, depressing sentiment on the stock. Concerns over capital spending on AI also played a role in the stock’s decline. Amazon.com’s outsize spending on AI initiatives caused some concern among investors, given its potential to dent profit margins. Macro concerns also weighed on the stock, including the prospect of worsened consumer sentiment leading to lower spending.

Agilent Technologies (A), a supplier of equipment and software for life sciences research, relies substantially on academic and government end markets, so recent cuts to grants from the National Institutes of Health impacted its near-term outlook.

Portfolio Positioning

Staying invested in quality franchises

Health Care, Information Technology and Communication Services are our top three sector overweights relative to the benchmark. Given a more concentrated portfolio, we generally expect the ETF to exhibit greater deviation from the benchmark during periods of high volatility. This was reflected in returns for the ETF this quarter. However, we believe the market sell-off also provides cleaner positioning for a rebound in quality stocks. We remain optimistic for companies we hold in the ETF to outperform over the long term.

The ETF's Health Care holdings include a mix of cyclical and defensive ideas. As the market sold off, our defensive Health Care names held their value and contributed to returns.

While the Health Care sector is currently experiencing regulatory uncertainty, we believe aging demographics and rising healthcare spending in developing countries make the long-term risk-reward attractive in the sector.

Our overweight in Information Technology is tied to cloud and AI adoption, where we continue to expect secular growth despite some near-term softness. The majority of our Communications Services exposure is tied to the telecommunication services industry, which helped the portfolio with downside protection during the quarter.

The ETF’s holdings remained relatively steady in the quarter. Only a few trades were made to recycle capital from early outperformers (such as Alphabet) to other names with more potential upside.

During this period of uncertainty, our focus is on opportunistically adding exposure to secular themes when we find bargains due to temporary reversals in investor sentiment. These areas of secular opportunity within the ETF include drug innovation, life science tools, managed care, AI, cloud, payments, e-commerce, agriculture mechanization and transport infrastructure.

Outlook

Focus on quality and valuation during this period of uncertainty

U.S. stocks suffered their worst first quarter since 2022 as sudden shifts in domestic regulatory priorities, government austerity and trade tensions widened the range of outcomes for the U.S. economy. After two consecutive years of double-digit returns, markets are now going through a period of adjustment as businesses and consumers evaluate an altered global macroeconomic landscape. The uncertain impact of tariffs on the prices of goods and services is also giving the Fed further pause on lowering interest rates. This is a notable change from market expectations at the time of U.S. elections last November. However, progress on the administration's priorities around tax cuts, deregulation and eventually lower rates could revive investor optimism.

We believe this heightened period of uncertainty should favor resilient businesses with strong market positions and create bargain opportunities that can reward long-term, patient investors. Roughly 90% of the Fund is invested in companies that have an economic moat, with roughly 60% invested in wide moat companies, as defined by Morningstar. These companies are likely to emerge stronger as they continue to invest to widen their competitive advantages.

Our fundamental bottom-up process involves ongoing assessment of downside risk and rotation of capital into companies that offer higher reward for lower risk. We focus on companies with durable competitive advantages and sustainable growth prospects, ensuring that our portfolio is well positioned to weather economic uncertainties. Our disciplined approach to investing in quality businesses at attractive valuations aims to deliver strong returns for our investors over the long term.

Important Information

PIL-718134-2025-04-09

The Russell 1000® Value Index is a market capitalization weighted index that measures the performance of those Russell 1000 companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth rates. An investor cannot invest directly in an index. The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of the large cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with relatively lower price-to-book ratios, lower I/B/E/S forecast medium term (2 year) growth and lower sales per share historical growth (5 years). The Russell 1000® Value Index is constructed to provide a comprehensive and unbiased barometer for the large cap value segment. The index is completely reconstituted annually to ensure new and growing equities are included and that the represented companies continue to reflect value characteristics.

1. As described in the Fund's current prospectus dated December 11, 2024, Parnassus Investments has contractually agreed to reduce its investment advisory fee to the extent necessary to limit total operating expenses to 0.59% of the Fund’s average daily net assets. This agreement will not be terminated prior to May 1, 2026, and may be continued indefinitely by the investment adviser on a year-to-year basis.

ENVIRONMENTAL, SOCIAL AND GOVERNANCE (ESG) GUIDELINES: The Fund evaluates ESG factors as part of the investment decision-making process, considering a range of impacts they may have on future revenues, expenses, assets, liabilities and overall risk. The Fund also utilizes active ownership to encourage more sustainable business policies and practices and greater ESG transparency. Active ownership strategies include proxy voting, dialogue with company management and sponsorship of shareholder resolutions, and public policy advocacy. There is no guarantee that the ESG strategy will be successful.

Investing involves risk and principal loss is possible. ETFs may trade at a premium or discount to NAV. Shares of any ETF are bought and sold at market prices (not NAV) and are not individually redeemed from the Fund. Brokerage commissions will reduce returns.

data.title: TSMC Q1 Preview: Beating Trump's Tariffs
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

Shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM) (OTC:TSMWF), (shorthand TSMC) are down roughly 21% since the last time I wrote on the key semiconductor company in January. At the time of my last piece of research, shares were worth just over $200. They've since dipped to the mid-$150s.

Despite this slight drop off, I remain super bullish on the firm. I believe that AI chip demand is still powering robust growth, which will only continue to push demand for their fab services higher. Looking at this from a bigger picture, shares have moved up 50% since the start of 2024. This is incredible growth and well ahead of the market. The company is still a compounder.

With this, TSMC needs to show investors how the future for them is Made in the USA. I touched on this before in some of my previous coverage from last year. TSMC needs to derisk their business from a Chinese invasion of Taiwan. U.S. plants are part of this diversification strategy. Now, however, these factories will likely become the heart of what gives this company a huge edge in the fab space.

In essence, their future is made in the USA.

While 2024 was a fruitful year for TSMC, I am still convinced that 2025 will be even stronger due to sustainable AI demand from some GPU designers and inference AI demand.

In my opinion, TSMC's fundamentals don't just still justify a strong buy rating, they also point to further upside. I believe the company is well-positioned heading into earnings Thursday.

Why I Am Doing Follow-Up Coverage

The main reason why I wanted follow-up coverage on TSMC is because recent developments point to the company fundamentally changing their supply chain and manufacturing strategy. To recap, at the tail end of 2024, the U.S. Commerce Department finalized a $6.6 billion government subsidy (plus up to $5 billion in low-cost government loans) for TSMC's production plant in Arizona. Most investors already knew about TSMC's play for CHIPS act funds. What is key (and what is changing) is their new focus on making US manufacturing the forefront of their future fabs (vs. an afterthought).

Recent announcements by both GPU giant NVIDIA (NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) mean that some of the best future GPUs that power key data centers will now be built here in the US, thanks to TSMC putting their fabs in states like Arizona.

With this, the company is now experiencing some of the best fab yields globally from their plants in Arizona. This has huge implications for where the US sits in the global semiconductor supply chain. It also means that it's far more likely that US investors reward the company's shares with a higher P/E multiple. I believe earnings will be a huge catalyst.

TSMC: Made In America

In January, TSMC opened its Phoenix plant, and progress appears to be incredible. In the three months that the plant has been in operation, the 4nm (4-nanometer) line is already yielding about 4% more usable chips than the company's Taiwanese counterparts. Not only does this early success in the U.S. offset higher local operating costs, but it also validates the broader expansion strategy.

I also think it benefits TSMC that they have begun to produce chips on U.S. soil to offset geopolitical risks.

Driven by policies like the 2022 CHIPS Act, which is allocating over $52 billion for domestic semiconductor production, directly supports TSMC's expansion. TSMC's Arizona facility is set to benefit from the $6.6 billion in federal grants I mentioned before. This will help accelerate plans for advanced nodes like 5nm today and eventually 2nm by 2028.

Advanced manufacturing at their Arizona facilities is not new on its own. What is new is the fact that these facilities are now beating their advanced rival facilities in Taiwan.

Just in time as well, the Trump administration announced this past week new tariffs on semiconductors, which will likely come later this year as part of their trade strategy to make more critical manufacturing items here in the U.S.

To offset this, some of TSMC's major customers like Nvidia are also using TSMC's Phoenix fab for its up-and-coming Blackwell AI chips. AMD is also moving to build more chips in the US to avoid any new tariff risks.

In essence, made in the USA is not just a government incentive play by TSMC. It's a fundamentally different approach for them to navigate a trade environment that is becoming more difficult to work in. For TSMC, the optimal strategy is to make more stuff in the US. Customers are rewarding them for this investment. I expect this to play a greater role in their supply chain strategy going forward.

Q1 Preview

TSMC is expected to report its Q1 earnings on Thursday, and given where revenue figures came in for Q1, the expectation is that the company will post strong earnings. As of right now, four analysts project that TSMC will finish the quarter with an EPS of $2.07. If this EPS is met, it would represent a 49.64% year-over-year increase.

Additionally, 21 analysts predict TSMC to bring in $25.77 billion in revenue this quarter, which would be a 41.37% year-over-year increase. Already, Q1 revenue has been reported at $25.6 billion by Reuters. So we should expect the final Q1 figure that will be reported as a part of Q1 to come closely in line with this.

TSMC's incredible growth in Q1 is largely fueled by rampant AI chip demand, with January-March sales coming in strong and slightly beating consensus estimates. I expect their new U.S. fab to only increase demand from here. Demand could even shift from competitor Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), given their US fab is not expected to be operational until next year.

Valuation

While TSMC has some incredible catalysts, the world's largest Semiconductor fab only has a forward Non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio of 17.38. When we compare this number to the sector median of 18.85, we see that this is actually a 7.78% discount. Seeking Alpha Quant gives TSMC a grade of a B- on this metric.

On this same front, TSMC has an incredibly attractive forward PEG ratio. Right now, TSMC's forward PEG ratio is at 0.58, which is a 58.26% discount to the sector median of 1.39. Seeking Alpha Quant gives TSMC a grade of an A on this metric. I really believe that the market is seriously underestimating Taiwan Semiconductor's forward growth.

With this, I think we should see TSMC shares trade at least at the sector median forward PEG ratio. If we saw this, this would represent 141.37% upside for the world's largest chip fab company. 141% may seem like a lot of upside. But to be clear, I think this is driven by investors appreciating the company's new made in the USA strategy and what that means for the company's operation stability.

Risks

I think the bull case for TSMC is super strong, but I will admit it's not risk-free.

I think the biggest risk is a potential AI GPU slowdown. I think there is some risk that some customers double-ordered or built up excessive inventories. If this happens, we could see Semi momentum halt later on in the second half of this year.

I think TSMC's biggest hedge for this is their US fabs. I believe we're likely to see tariffs on semis from this administration. TSMC's fabs in Arizona should have incredible pricing power going forward as a result of this. With this, on the operations side, I remain a little skeptical on the overall success of the Arizona fab ramp-up. As I mentioned earlier, the Phoenix fab has already seen incredible success, but it is still early. The fab has only been in operation for about three months, which means that there isn't a large sample size to pull from. Labor shortages (because this is specialty manufacturing) have plagued the US semi space in general. TSMC will likely have a supply side problem vs. a demand side.

Even then, I think it's important to recognize that roughly 90% of their production is still coming from Taiwan. In the long run, because there are a lot of tensions between China and Taiwan, they are going to have to spend a ton on Capex to help offset this risk. An invasion of Taiwan is more on the black-swan side of the risk bell curve. That doesn't mean it's impossible. My valuation estimates for the company leave the firm at the sector median forward PEG ratio. If we could see more of their production take place outside Taiwan, then I think their PEG ratio should be meaningfully higher than the sector median.

Bottom Line

While shares have fallen off over 20% YTD, I remain a strong buy on shares of TSMC.

While the AI boom is still one of the biggest trades on the market, the world's largest fab company is still riding incredible tailwinds driven by the AI boom, and their leadership in advanced nodes (particularly 3nm). Now, with the firm getting serious about U.S. production capacity, I think that the company is in an even greater spot. The company is really firing on all cylinders. There's a lot of room for shares to move up from here.

TSMC has proven that it is able to provide a consistent record of delivering strong earnings beats and robust top line growth. Top line growth is already evident for Q1, with a 42% revenue spike in Q1. Its growing U.S. footprint doesn't just enhance political support, but it also delivers critical wins - like Nvidia's decision to produce AI chips in America.

The company is also trading at very attractive multiples in comparison to its peers. I think the current valuation mismatch provides significant upside potential for investors willing to be in a Taiwan company that is becoming more American with every semiconductor it produces.

With this, shares remain a strong buy heading into the Q1 report.

data.title: DiaMedica Therapeutics: Stroke Developer With Low Cash Reserve
data.description:
This is my first DiaMedica Therapeutics (NASDAQ:DMAC) article. DiaMedica is a clinical stage biopharmaceutical company with one primary product in clinical development for two indications. These are:

  1. DM199 in a Pivotal Phase 2/3 trial for the treatment of acute ischemic stroke [AIS] and
  2. DM199 in a Phase 2 trial for Preeclampsia.

In this article, I will reference DiaMedica's Q4, 2024 business update financial results press release, earnings conference call and 10-K, all released on 3/17/2025.

DiaMedica's lead DM199 indication has an intriguing profile.

General

In its 10-K business overview addressing its lead candidate DM199 (rinvecalinase alfa) therapy in AIS, DiaMedica characterizes it as:

  1. the first pharmaceutically active synthetic form of the human tissue kallikrein-1 (rhKLK1) protein to be clinically studied in patients;
  2. granted Fast Track Designation by FDA;
  3. kallikrein-1 (KLK1), extracted from human urine, is an established therapeutic modality in Asia for the treatment of AIS;
  4. being synthetic eliminates concerns about pathogen transmission.

An 01/2025 peer-reviewed publication described in the conference call provides scientific insight into:

...DM199's mechanism for increasing collateral circulation and salvaging brain tissue at risk from infarction following an AIS.

AIS

Aged and aging worthies such as myself who are aware of the F-A-S-T stroke recognition acronym may despair at ever getting treatment within the recommended time. Even if a person gets to the hospital on a timely basis, there are often delays in getting proper treatment.

The problem is that stroke treatments typically work best when administered soon after stroke onset. DM199 is working to change this, extending the time within which treatment may be effective from a few hours to up to 24 hours.

Slide 27 above from DiaMedica's 04/2025 Corporate Presentation shows how this dramatically improves the percentage of patients who can benefit from therapy. It posits that 90% of patients can reach the emergency department within 24 hours compared to 20% who can within 4.5 hours to qualify for tPA the only FDA approved therapy.

Details of trial

DiaMedica dubbed its phase 2/3 AIS trial ReMEDy2. It characterizes it as an:

...adaptive design, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial studying the use of the Company’s product candidate, DM199, to treat acute ischemic stroke (AIS) patients. Patients enrolled in the trial will be treated for three weeks with either DM199 or placebo, beginning within 24 hours of the onset of AIS symptoms, with the final follow-up at 90 days.

It dosed its first US patient in 04/2024. Its clinicaltrials.gov identifier is NCT05065216.

The study completion is listed as anticipated in 12/2026. A formal interim analysis will be conducted after 200 participants complete their Day 90 assessment. The purposes of this interim analysis are to assess safety, allow early stopping of the study for futility, or continue the study with a revised final sample up to a maximum of 728 participants.

During the earnings call, CMO Masuoka discussed the challenges DiaMedica was facing in achieving a critical mass of sites to generate a steady stream of enrollments.

Competition

The 10-K describes the competitive landscape. There is only 1 FDA approved therapy for AIS; it is tPA, brand name Activase; its therapeutic window is limited to up to 4.5 hours after onset of the AIS. Presentation slides 26 and 37 estimate the size of the AIS opportunity in the US as from $5 billion to >$10 billion.

Unsurprisingly, this enormous potential market has bred significant competition from pharmas of all sizes around the world. The 10-K lists 11 different pharmaceuticals currently in various stages of development. It seems quite likely that by the time DM199 is approved it will face an entirely different, more competitive, market than that prevailing in 04/2025.

DiaMedica maintains a low liquidity profile with modest cash burn and a short cash runway

During the earnings call, CFO Kellen reported that DiaMedica's cash and investments at the close of 12/31/2024 totaled ~$44.1 million. Its cash burn as reflected by its net cash used in operating activities for 2024 was ~$22.1 million, compared to ~$18.7 million for 2023.

The year-over-year increase in burn was driven by:

... the combination of our increased net loss and the advance of deposit funds to vendors supporting our ReMEDy2 trial during 2024. These were partially offset by changes in operating assets and liabilities during 2024, particularly the increase in accrued liabilities related to our ReMEDy2 trial and ongoing manufacturing development activities as of December 31, 2024.

When going back to review its 10-Q quarterly cash burns of $6.6 million for Q1, 2024, $6.4 million for Q2, 2024 and $9.4 million for Q3, 2024 it shows a net cash increase of $0.3 million for Q4, 2024.

Kellen indicated that management's anticipated cash runway as of 12/31/2024 ran into Q3, 2026. Its stock has had a rough several months since its halcyon trading above $6.00 late in 12/2024.

It traded above $6.00 for several periods from 12/2024 until as late as 3/18/2025. As I write on 04/15/2025 it trades at $3.59. It has only traded above $4.00 on a single day so far in April 2025. The lesson I take from this is that management needs to have things set up to make hay while the sun shines.

We are currently in a period of great uncertainty. The environment could hardly be worse for a little biotech like DiaMedica to raise cash. It would have been far better if management had been prepared to offer stock to the market before it needed to.

Limited near term catalysts make DiaMedica a high risk speculative "Hold"

DiaMedica's Presentation overview slide 4 below provides a snapshot of the company's key pipeline assets:

DiaMedica is working towards approval in two high-value indications. The four Wall Street Analysts who follow the stock have an average price target of $8.75 for an upside of ~156%. Therein lies the bull case.

I am more wary of the stock. It does not have any apparent market moving near term positive catalysts. Its cash runway means that it will soon need to raise additional funds, creating a significant near term negative catalyst.

Conclusion

I assess DiaMedica as having great potential over the mid to long term, if all goes well with its clinical trials. That is always a big if with clinical stage biotechs. Over the near term, DiaMedica is unattractive. In today's high-risk tariff infused market, it is not one that is worthwhile with an unproven biotech that has a cash runway that barely extends longer than a year.

data.title: Empire State Manufacturing Survey: Activity Continued To Contract In April
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Manufacturing activity contracted for a second consecutive month in New York State, according to the Empire State Manufacturing April survey. The diffusion index for General Business Conditions rose 11.9 points but remained below zero at -8.1. The latest reading was better than the forecast of -12.8.

Here are details from the report.

ACTIVITY CONTINUES TO CONTRACT

Manufacturing activity fell for a second consecutive month in New York State, according to the April survey. After dropping twenty-six points last month, the general business conditions index climbed twelve points but remained below zero at -8.1. The new orders and shipments indexes also held below zero at -8.8 and -2.9, respectively, pointing to ongoing declines in both orders and shipments. Unfilled orders edged up slightly. The inventories index came in at 7.4, signaling that business inventories continued to expand. Delivery times were unchanged, while the supply availability index fell to -5.7, suggesting supply availability was somewhat lower.

PRICE INCREASES ACCELERATE

The index for number of employees came in at -2.6, while the average workweek index fell to -9.1, pointing to little change in employment levels but a decline in hours worked. Both price indexes climbed for a fourth consecutive month to their highest levels in more than two years: the prices paid index rose six points to 50.8, and the prices received index rose six points to 28.7.

Background on the Empire State Manufacturing Survey

The Empire State Manufacturing Index rates the relative level of general business conditions in New York state. A level above 0.0 indicates improving conditions, and below indicates worsening conditions. The reading is compiled from a survey of about 200 manufacturers in New York state.

Below is a chart of the current conditions and its 3-month moving average, which helps clarify the trend for this extremely volatile indicator. The current 3-month moving average stands at -7.5.

Since this survey only goes back to July of 2001, we only have two complete business cycles with which to evaluate its usefulness as an indicator for the broader economy. Following the great recession, the index has slipped into contraction multiple times, as the general trend slowed.

We saw a gradual decline in 2015 that rose back up in 2016, with a giant dip in 2020 due to COVID-19. The index quickly picked up again in 2021, declined for 2022, and gradually rose in 2023.

The index kicked off 2024 with a sharp decline but rose during the back half of the year, reaching positive territory for the first time in over 2.5 years. However, the first few months of 2025 have pulled the index back into negative territory.

Here is an overlay of the current and future conditions (a six-month outlook).

FIRMS TURN PESSIMISTICFirms expect conditions to worsen in the months ahead, a level of pessimism that has only occurred a handful of times in the history of the survey. The index for future general business conditions fell twenty points to -7.4; the index has fallen a cumulative forty-four points over the past three months. New orders and shipments are expected to fall slightly in the months ahead. Capital spending plans were flat. Input and selling price increases are expected to pick up, and supply availability is expected to worsen over the next six months.

Meanwhile, here's another look at the latest ISM Manufacturing Business Activity Index.

Let's compare all five regional manufacturing indicators. Here is a three-month moving average overlay of each since 2004 (for those with data).

Here is the same chart, including the average of the five. Readers will notice the range in expansion and contraction among all regions.

ETFs associated with industrials and manufacturing include: First Trust Industrials/Producer Durables AlphaDEX Fund (FXR), Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLI), Vanguard Industrials ETF (VIS), and iShares U.S. Industrials ETF (IYJ).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: NY Fed Shows Weak Demand And Flying Prices
data.description:
On Tuesday morning, we got the first update on regional manufacturing activity for April with the release of the New York Fed's Empire State Manufacturing Survey. The headline reading came in better than expected, rising from a 15-month low of -20 last month to -8.1 in April.

While that marks a second straight month of observed contractionary activity, expectations appear even worse. That index fell an enormous 20.1 points month over month for the second-lowest reading in the survey's history. That month that was worse: September 2001. Regarding the month-over-month move, September 2001 and March 2020 are the only other months with bigger drops.

In the table below, we include a look at the April and March readings across all categories of the report. We include the sequential change and how those all rank in percentiles. As shown, breadth and levels were not too bad for current conditions whereas six-month expectations look disastrous including a handful of record lows and bottom quintile monthly declines.

To quantify the drops across those expectation categories, below we have taken a standard deviation for each category and then averaged across them. As shown, that reading dropped into negative territory this month.

In the post-pandemic period, there have been a couple of other months to also see negative readings (in July and November 2022), although this current reading is considerably lower.

As such, April's reading of -0.12 is the worst since April 2020, and before that, only the Great Recession and 2001 saw weaker expectations. Likewise, the more than one standard deviation drop over the past three months marks one of the most rapid declines in expectations on record.

As noted earlier, the drop in expectations indices has led to record lows in some. That applies to new orders and shipment expectations, with current condition indices for both categories also remaining in contraction. That would indicate the region's firms have been observing softening demand, and things are only expected to get worse.

It's not exactly a silver lining, but not every category has fallen sharply. Two categories in the upper quintile of readings with sharp moves higher in the past month were inflation-related. Prices paid and prices received are both rocketing higher in terms of current conditions and expectations.

For current conditions of both categories, this was the most elevated level since August 2022. As for expectations, it was the highest level for prices paid since June 2022, and April 2022 for prices received.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Netflix Earnings Preview: Everyone's Bullish, But Tough Compares In 2025
data.description:
Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX) reports their first quarter, ’25 after the closing bell on Thursday, April 17th, 2025, with Street consensus expecting $10.5 billion in expected revenue growth, $3 billion in operating income and $5.72 in earnings per share (EPS) for expected year-over-year growth of 12%, 14% and 5% respectively.

Netflix, even with the expected price hike this quarter (the latest Netflix price increase just came through this month), faces very tough compares in 2025.

Here’s how the year-over-year (y-o-y) growth numbers looked in 2024:

  • Q1 ’24 revenue, op inc and EPS: 15%, 54% and 91%
  • Q2 ’24 revenue, op inc and EPS: 17%, 38%, and 48%
  • Q3 ’24 revenue, op inc and EPS: 15%, 52% and 45%
  • Q4 ’24 revenue, op inc and EPS: 16%, 52% and 102%

While expected revenue for Q1 ’25 is inline with previous quarters, both operating income and expected EPS growth for Q1 ’25 is well below 2024’s actual growth rates.

Q4 ’24 results, released in mid-January ’25, showed that the Q1 ’25 guidance was a little light at the same time Netflix raised revenue guidance for full-year 2025. (According to the notes (unsure of the source), 55% of the new member sign-ups came in countries with the tiered-ad plan. The 4th quarter saw the NFL games on Christmas Day, the Squid Games, and the Jake Paul – Mike Tyson fight.

Have to wonder what Netflix has planned for Q4 ’25.

Q2 ’25 per the current consensus is expecting $10.9 billion in revenue and $6.27 in EPS for expected growth of 14% and 28% respectively.

Netflix EPS revision trends

Netflix revenue revisions trends

Both EPS and revenue estimate trends remain positive for Netflix, but the increase in EPS is more noticeable thanks to Netflix’s improving operating margin. The Netflix operating margin has improved from the low 20% range in 2023 to 30% by the end of ’24, probably much of that due to Netflix’s pricing lower.

Valuation

With a growth stock, valuation is usually the first negative cited, and Netflix is no exception.

Trading at 40x expected ’25 EPS that is expected to grow at 25%, the PE-to-growth ratio is probably a little smaller than expected, possibly due to the market turmoil since March 31, ’25, but it still leaves plenty of downside should there be anything in the earnings report that gives investors pause about long-term growth.

Preferring to look at the metrics over a 3-year forward time frame, EPS is expected to grow (average) 22% over the next three years, with an average PE of 33x on 12% average 3-year expected revenue growth.

What looks even worse is the 55-60x cash flow and free cash metrics, which is somewhat understandable as Netflix continues to invest in sports programming. There has been a tremendous improvement in Netflix’s cash generation since Covid when Netflix was free cash flow negative for a period of years, but since 2022, Netflix's free cash flow generation has improved dramatically.

This blog’s intrinsic value estimate on Netflix is $1,050, while Morningstar has an estimate of $700 per share. In late 2021, Netflix peaked at $700 per share, and fell all the way to $160 by mid-2022 in the midst of the fed funds rate increases that year.

Summary/conclusion

Netflix had a remarkable 4th quarter ’24 thanks to the NFL games, etc. Although on a smaller scale, Netflix's capex spend seems to resemble Amazon (AMZN) a little bit as the streaming giant tempers capex, and then when it sees an opportunity like the Jake Paul – Tyson match, and the NFL games on Christmas Day, capex ramps to 2x what it was in Q4 ’23.

The stock returned 84% in calendar ’24, so this blog’s Q4 ’24 earnings preview was probably too conservative, since the stock jumped sharply following earnings.

Without question, Netflix’s largest competitive advantage is the lead they have on their closest competitors like Disney (DIS) and Amazon. As a Prime subscriber, I occasionally watch Prime streaming, and while there are some decent shows, it pales in comparison to Netflix.

Netflix has been a top 10 holding for clients, but after 2024’s gain, about 10% of the shares held have been sold, just to make sure that Netflix’s weight within client accounts doesn’t get too large.

There is a lot of bullishness surrounding Netflix coming into Wednesday night’s report, which is unusual given the market instability and uncertainty. The recent Wall Street Journal article and a lot of other articles have talked positively around the stock, given any lack of tariff impact on Netflix’s business.

Frankly, I’d rather see a lot of bad news baked into a stock prior to earnings, than good news, since it limits the downside of any disappointment.

There is that $700 gap still outstanding and looming from what looks to be the October ’24 earnings report, but in some cases those gaps never get filled.

Readers should know that there is significant downside with a name like Netflix, should earnings disappoint for any reason.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Investing can and does involve the loss of principal, even for short periods of time. EPS and revenue estimates are sourced from LSEG.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Ericsson Jumps On Attractive Results, And Shares Deserve To Rise Further
data.description:
April 15th ended up being a pretty good day for shareholders of Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (publ) (NASDAQ:ERIC), or Ericsson for short. The global provider of mobile connectivity solutions saw its share price rise by roughly 8.3% after management announced financial results covering the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year.

There are reasons to be cautious moving forward according to management. However, the market was pleasantly surprised with the fact that the company exceeded analysts’ expectations when it came to revenue and earnings per share. The picture is going so well, in fact, that management even announced a special dividend.

In the past, the company has seen rather significant volatility because of industry spending when it comes to global 5G rollout. These uncertain times are practically guaranteed to cause that volatility to continue. But through it all, the firm has consistently generated attractive cash flow and shares are trading at levels that I can only say are attractive to me. So even though it might be a bumpy ride moving forward, I do believe that this enterprise warrants a soft "Buy" rating at this time.

A necessary disclosure

Most of the companies that I write about are based out of the U.S. However, Ericsson is different. The company can trace its roots back to its founding in Sweden way back in 1876. Its financial results are, as a result, in Swedish króna. Seeing as how the vast majority of my readership is likely more familiar with U.S. dollars as opposed to any other currency, this article relies on converting Swedish króna into dollars at the current exchange rate. I believe that this makes the content more digestible for a larger audience.

An interesting past

The management team at Ericsson describes the company as a leading provider of mobile connectivity solutions with operations that span the world. In fact, the business has operations spread across over 175 countries. The business even has a physical presence, by means of a joint venture with LG that it owns 75% of, in North Korea. During the company's 2024 fiscal year, 29% of its revenue came from North America, with the largest chunk of this coming from the U.S. All told, about 39.6% of the firm's revenue is attributable to the U.S.

This might seem incorrect considering that a smaller portion of its revenue is attributable to North America. However, the U.S.-based revenue includes certain licensing revenue that is based not on the market area that the sales come from, but are instead based on the location of its customer. Outside of North America, Europe and Latin America is the next largest collection of revenue, accounting for 26% of overall sales. However, the company does have significant other sales attributable to parts of Asia, the Middle East, and Africa.

To really understand the firm, it would be best to break it down into its different operating segments. The largest of these, by far, is its Networks segment. This accounts for roughly 64% of sales and is responsible for providing hardware and software solutions, as well as related service offerings, to help communications service providers as they continue to adopt 5G. Examples of products and other solutions offered under this segment include RAN (Radio Access Network) offerings like antennas, radios, software, and more. It includes indoor and outdoor RAN units for computational purposes. It involves fiber or microwave connections that connect RAN nodes with the Core making it possible to facilitate 5G mobile networks. The company also provides certain deployment and customer support solutions under this unit.

Next in line, accounting for 25% of sales would be the company’s Cloud Software and Services segment. Through this unit, the company provides solutions that help to equip communications service providers with the capabilities that they need as we move into a more cloud-based society. They offer, for instance, Core Networks which transfer network traffic at high speeds. They offer certain business and operations support systems, as well as managed network services.

The company generates about 10% of its revenue from its Enterprise segment. This part of the business focuses on providing advanced connectivity solutions that help customers accelerate digitalization. The goal here is to help customers, through solutions like its Global Communications Platform, to drive developer engagement and accelerate mobile-first digital transformation at an enterprise level. The firm also offers up certain wireless connectivity networks dedicated to things like vehicles, IoT devices, and more.

Over the last few years, Ericsson has seen extreme volatility in its financial performance. Revenue has actually dropped consistently, falling from $27.42 billion in 2022 to $25.03 billion in 2024. This might seem shocking. However, when we look beneath the hood, we can see what all is going on. The problem child for the company has been its Networks segment, with sales declining from $19.54 billion to $15.98 billion. Within this segment, revenue dedicated to North America plummeted from $8.27 billion in 2022 to $4.51 billion in 2023. Management attributed this to ‘record-setting’ investments made by companies in North America during both 2021 and 2022. After all, the push toward 5G has been a big thing here at home.

At some point, you would expect this to decline. It doesn't help that interest rates have increased quite a bit in order to combat inflationary pressures. High interest rates make big capital spending projects more costly, and inflation certainly didn't do the industry any favors. A rebound in spending in the U.S. helped to push revenue from North America up to $5.85 billion in 2024.

In Northeast Asia, revenue has been consistently falling, dropping from $2.28 billion to $1.46 billion over the last three years. Management blamed the decline in revenue for the region for reduced customer investments in certain 5G frontrunner markets. This basically means that some markets within that region invested heavily early and have since needed the services of a company like Ericsson less.

It is worth noting that this was not related only to the Networks segment. Because of a change in the timing of project deliverables and some changes in the scope of low-margin contracts, the firm’s Cloud Software and Services segment saw revenue decline from $477 million to $418 million. This is counter to what North America saw, with the Cloud Software and Services segment experiencing growth year after year, rising from $1.35 billion to $1.45 billion over the span of three years.

The Southeast Asia, Oceania, and India region for the company has also seen tremendous volatility. Because of a surge of investment in 2023 that did not repeat in 2024, we saw Networks revenue shoot up from $2.39 billion in 2022 to $4.37 billion in 2023. It then plummeted to $2.35 billion in 2024. Even the Cloud Software and Services segment reported a drop from $1.01 billion in 2023 to $920 million last year.

*Networks Revenue.

Despite the weakness that we saw in some key areas, other parts of the company have been faring better. From 2022 to 2024, Ericsson saw its Cloud Software and Services segment grow revenue from $6.11 billion to $6.33 billion. Furthermore, its Enterprise segment reported growth from $1.47 billion to $2.51 billion. Admittedly, both of those segments were a bit weaker in 2024 compared to 2023. But the declines were only marginal compared to the Networks segment.

*Cloud Software and Services Revenue.

Two specific countries caused the greatest volatility for the company. The U.S. was one of them, with revenue plummeting from $11.08 billion in 2022 to $8.62 billion in 2023. But because of a resurgence in investment in all things digital, revenue rebounded to $9.92 billion last year. In India, we saw a near tripling in revenue from $1.11 billion in 2022 to $3.15 billion in 2023. That was then roughly halved to $1.53 billion last year.

On the bottom line, the company has seen generally positive performance where it matters most. Even though the business went from generating net profits of $1.93 billion in 2022 to a net loss of $2.64 billion in 2023, it saw a recovery, generating $38 million in net income last year. Operating cash flow has been all over the map. But the $4.80 billion generated by the company in 2024 was the highest it had seen in at least three years. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $2.04 billion in 2023 to $2.49 billion last year.

For context, I did make a couple of adjustments to both of these cash flow figures to make them fall more in alignment with GAAP accounting as opposed to IFRS. Instead of reporting EBITDA, management emphasizes EBITA. After dropping from $2.98 billion in 2022 to $2.17 billion last year, there was a jump to $2.74 billion in 2024.

As I mentioned at the start of this article, the reason why shares appreciated so much on April 15th had to do with the financial results that management reported for the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Overall revenue was $5.56 billion. This represented an increase of 3.2% compared to the $5.39 billion reported one year earlier. It also happened to be $12.6 million greater than what analysts were anticipating.

The only operating segment for the company to grow during this time was its Networks segment, with revenue jumping 5.7% from $3.41 billion to $3.60 billion. Outside of the Americas, which reported continued growth as technology giants continue to invest heavily in all things digital like 5G, cloud computing, and more, the company experienced investment levels that were low.

Considering all that is going on with the global economy, and the fact that some markets like India were front-loaded in 2021 and 2022, I'm not surprised by this development. In comments related to these financial results, management said that they remain confident in their strong market position when it comes to mobile networks. They also expect their enterprise operations to stabilize throughout this year. They acknowledged that the global trade landscape and other macroeconomic concerns have created volatility and uncertainty and specified that they are not immune to all of this.

They did say, however, that they are resilient to changing market conditions because of their business model, and presumably because of their geographic diversity. They also touted the fact that, during the first quarter of the year, they were able to announce their first Asia Pacific programmable network that will include the deployment of what they call 5G Advanced. The only real guidance that they provided was that revenue growth seen on a year-over-year basis for both their Networks and Cloud Software and Services segments should be similar to what the company saw on a three-year average basis.

Profitability for the company was largely positive. Net income shot up from $264 million to $426 million. This increase came in spite of the fact that the company saw restructuring expenses grow from $21 million to $28 million. Management cautioned that restructuring expenses will remain elevated this year, though they did not say how much investors should expect. For context, last year, the company allocated $506 million toward restructuring initiatives. We did see a decline in operating cash flow from $634 million to $450 million. But on an adjusted basis, the metric improved from $560 million to $736 million.

Lastly, EBITA for the business increased from $515 million to $700 million. On a per share basis, management reported a profit of $0.13. That was up from the $0.08 per share reported the previous year and exceeded analysts’ estimates by $0.06. Management is confident enough in the profitability situation of the company that they have also proposed a special dividend for investors. On March 25th, the firm approved a dividend of about $0.29 per share, with $0.14 per share having been paid on April 1st and with the rest expected to be paid out on October 2nd.

In the absence of more detailed estimates for the year, the best way to value the company would be to use historical results for the last two fiscal years. In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced using these figures. These are low multiples, especially for a firm that has a net cash position of $1.44 billion. Broader economic uncertainty, combined with the historical volatility of the company can probably explain, at least to some extent, why ERIC shares are as cheap as they are.

Long term, the company certainly has a catalyst that should work in its favor. In the image above, you can see that, excluding mainland China, the world has achieved only about 40% of 5G mid-band coverage. By the year 2030, this picture will change, with the total number of global 5G mobile subscriptions expected to hit 6.3 billion. That's almost triple the 2.3 billion that the company estimated the world had at the end of 2024.

Furthermore, 5G will not be the final network that ever gets deployed. Management actually expects 6G to arrive around the year 2030. This should follow standalone 5G and 5G Advanced rollouts. In essence, it seems the company should have a perpetual cycle of growth ahead for it as technology improves.

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I must say that I am a fan of Ericsson. I don't like the volatility that the company has seen, but shares are attractively priced, and recent performance has been encouraging. The catalyst for growth for the company should propel it forward as time goes on as well. Given these factors, and despite certain headwinds such as global trade issues, inflation, and high interest rates, I believe that the enterprise warrants a soft "Buy" rating at this time.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: USRT: Binary Outcomes Based On Possible Nadir In CPI (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The iShares Core U.S. REIT ETF (NYSEARCA:USRT) is a fund that we last covered imagining that at least the supply chain issues with the Ukraine war would open up for at least some rate cuts. Those rate cuts came, but have stalled for the foreseeable future to the detriment of rate-sensitive investments like those specialized real estate investments within USRT. Incrementally, we are concerned with the residential REIT exposures, as unemployment rates slowly climb and the risks associated with a sort of stagflation that may come from the themes of deglobalization and trade wars that hurt productivity and bring in malignant inflation.

While the latest CPI figures were quite cool and very encouraging, and it would be fantastic for the U.S. economic prospects if CPI stayed that way, inflation expectations point inflation in the other direction. But the lower oil price should also be a help.

USRT Breakdown

Exposure is meaningful to REITs like Prologis, Inc. (PLD), which we'd consider to be specialty REITs in the world of logistics real estate. Performance hasn't been particularly good, but incrementally we aren't too concerned with tenancies and lease conditions in this market. Indeed, revenues and net incomes have only climbed for PLD reflecting that it's all about costs of capital and related valuation. Otherwise, exposures are to old age homes and retirement communities in healthcare REITs, similarly defensive on the demand side. Other more resilient segments might be data centers, among other specialized REIT categories.

The concerns on the demand side are around the other types of real estate:

  • Retail REITs are clearly exposed to economic conditions. Leases are shorter here and foot traffic and the extent of consumer activity will be important to the value of this category.
  • Multifamily and self-storage REITs are exposed to the employment rates and people's staying power in these leveraged markets.
  • Office REITs may have longer leases, but this is already an upset market segment and economic conditions matter quite a bit for the value of office real estate leases. However, the rebalancing of work from home versus return to office has been a decent development for office real estate stocks on the back of improving office lease terms.

The concerns we have about the macro environment are reflected in the downward translation and the yield curve inversion over the next 4 years. Markets are concerned that the tariffs are going to impact the high-rate consumers constituting the U.S. consumer market due to an erosion of purchasing power as tariffs continue to escalate on Chinese goods, heavily exported to the U.S. in a reflection of the trade imbalance.

Similarly, with purchasing power in question due to inflation, and higher inflation expectations than even in most of 2022, we do not think that on the valuation side costs of capital will support valuations incrementally with rates tending up in the longer term more relevant for real estate, though inverted in the shorter term reflecting market concerns about the economy and the possible need to cut rates to address growth issues.

Bottom Line

While the USRT is a broad and representative ETF that only carries a 0.08% expense ratio, the cost of capital issue in particular on the higher inflation expectations is a considerable concern. However, we must comment that in the latest CPI readings, inflation had actually cooled to 2.4%. This is impressive considering oil comps were gradually toughening, with prices stalled at around $70-74 per barrel.

While we are concerned with the next reading in connection to expectations, we also have to note that another deflationary vector has been the even lower oil prices as a consequence of economic concerns in the wake of the trade wars and the initial reciprocal tariff announcement now suspended. The rule of thumb is a $10 per barrel decline can reduce inflation between 0.2-0.4% as measured by CPI. We've got around a $10 decline (CL1:COM) in the last month.

We think that CPI outcomes and therefore cost of capital assumptions that matter a lot for USRT and REITs could either surprise considerably to the upside or the downside, therefore a bit binary. In general, we focus on inflation expectations because it is a leading indicator but also a causative factor for inflation. No other metric is more important than inflation expectations, though the readings are only as good as the Michigan survey. Pass on USRT.

data.title: Hancock Whitney Corporation 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Hancock Whitney Corporation in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.
